title: In hashing, functions "insert" and "find" have the same time com
content:
In hashing, functions "insert" and "find" have the same time complexity.
~@[](1)
author: 冯雁
description: In hashing, functions "insert" and "find" have the same time complexity.

title: Linear probing isn't equivalent to double hashing with a seconda
content: Linear probing isn't equivalent to double hashing with a secondary hash function of $Hash _2(k)$=1.
author: 朱建科
description: Linear probing isn't equivalent to double hashing with a secondary hash function of $Hash _2(k)$=1.

title: Store $$M$$ elements in a hash table which is represented by an
content:
Store $$M$$ elements in a hash table which is represented by an array of size $$S$$, the loading density is then $$M/S$$. 
~@[](1)
author: DS课程组
description: Store $$M$$ elements in a hash table which is represented by an array of size $$S$$, the loading density is then $$M/S$$.

title: The average search time of searching a hash table with $$N$$ ele
content:
The average search time of searching a hash table with $$N$$ elements is: @[](1)

A. $$O(1)$$
B. $$O(logN)$$
C. $$O(N)$$
D. cannot be determined
author: DS课程组
description: The average search time of searching a hash table with $$N$$ elements is:

choices: the expected number of probes for insertions is greater than that for successful searches in linear probing method
if the table size is prime and the table is at least half empty, a new element can always be inserted with quadratic probing
in separate chaining method, if duplicate elements are allowed in the list, insertions are generally quicker than deletions

title: Given an initially empty hash table `HT` with length 7, together
content:
Given an initially empty hash table `HT` with length 7, together with a hash function $$H(k)=k\% 7$$.  Let us use linear probing to solve collisions.  What is the average search length for successful searches after inserting 22, 43, 15 one by one into `HT`?  @[](2)

A. 1.5
B. 1.6
C. 2
D. 3
author: 考研真题
description: Given an initially empty hash table `HT` with length 7, together with a hash function $$H(k)=k\% 7$$.  Let us use linear probing to solve collisions.  What is the average search length for successful searches after inserting 22, 43, 15 one by one into `HT`?

choices: Everything else are the same for the two elements except their keys
Two elements have different data but share the same key
Two elements with different keys share the same hash value
Two elements with different hash values share the same key

title: What is a collision in hashing?
content:
What is a collision in hashing? @[](1)

A. Everything else are the same for the two elements except their keys
B. Two elements have different data but share the same key
C. Two elements with different keys share the same hash value
D. Two elements with different hash values share the same key
author: DS课程组
description: What is a collision in hashing?

title: Insert {18, 23, 11, 20, 2, 7, 27, 33, 42, 15} one by one into an
content:
Insert {18, 23, 11, 20, 2, 7, 27, 33, 42, 15} one by one into an initially empty hash table of size 11 with the hash function $$H(Key) = Key\%11$$, and linear probing is used to resolve collisions.  What is the loading density when the first collision occurs? @[](3)

A. 0.27
B. 0.45
C. 0.64
D. 0.73
author: DS课程组
description: Insert {18, 23, 11, 20, 2, 7, 27, 33, 42, 15} one by one into an initially empty hash table of size 11 with the hash function $$H(Key) = Key\%11$$, and linear probing is used to resolve collisions.  What is the loading density when the first collision occurs?

title: Suppose that the range of a hash table is [0, 18], and the hash
content:
Suppose that the range of a hash table is [0, 18], and the hash function is H(Key)=Key%17. If linear probing is used to resolve collisions, then after inserting { 16, 32, 14, 34, 48 } one by one into the hash table, the index of 48 is:   @[](2)

A. 14
B. 0
C. 17
D. 1
author: 陈越
description: Suppose that the range of a hash table is [0, 18], and the hash function is H(Key)=Key%17. If linear probing is used to resolve collisions, then after inserting { 16, 32, 14, 34, 48 } one by one into the hash table, the index of 48 is:

title: Hashing - Hard Version
content:
Given a hash table of size $$N$$, we can define a hash function $$H(x) = x\%N$$.  Suppose that the linear probing is used to solve collisions, we can easily obtain the status of the hash table with a given sequence of input numbers.

However, now you are asked to solve the reversed problem: reconstruct the input sequence from the given status of the hash table.  Whenever there are multiple choices, the smallest number is always taken.

### Input Specification:

Each input file contains one test case.  For each test case, the first line contains a positive integer $$N$$ ($$\le 1000$$), which is the size of the hash table.  The next line contains $$N$$ integers, separated by a space.  A negative integer represents an empty cell in the hash table.  It is guaranteed that all the non-negative integers are distinct in the table.

### Output Specification:

For each test case, print a line that contains the input sequence, with the numbers separated by a space.  Notice that there must be no extra space at the end of each line.

### Sample Input:
```in
11
33 1 13 12 34 38 27 22 32 -1 21
```

### Sample Output:
```out
1 13 12 21 33 34 38 27 22 32
```
author: 何钦铭
description:
Given a hash table of size $$N$$, we can define a hash function $$H(x) = x\%N$$.  Suppose that the linear probing is used to solve collisions, we can easily obtain the status of the hash table with a given sequence of input numbers.

However, now you are asked to solve the reversed problem: reconstruct the input sequence from the given status of the hash table.  Whenever there are multiple choices, the smallest number is always taken.

### Input Specification:

Each input file contains one test case.  For each test case, the first line contains a positive integer $$N$$ ($$\le 1000$$), which is the size of the hash table.  The next line contains $$N$$ integers, separated by a space.  A negative integer represents an empty cell in the hash table.  It is guaranteed that all the non-negative integers are distinct in the table.

### Output Specification:

For each test case, print a line that contains the input sequence, with the numbers separated by a space.  Notice that there must be no extra space at the end of each line.

### Sample Input:
```in
11
33 1 13 12 34 38 27 22 32 -1 21
```

### Sample Output:
```out
1 13 12 21 33 34 38 27 22 32
```
